marketOverview: Markets enter the week in a dramatically different posture compared to last week. The US-Iran 2-week ceasefire, announced Wednesday April 8, triggered a violent cross-asset reversal: Brent collapsed from $116/bbl to below $90/bbl, DXY fell over 1.5%, equities surged (SPX +2.9%, NDX +3.4%), and high-beta currencies (NZD, AUD, GBP) led the G10 board. However, the ceasefire is fragile — Hormuz shipping remains near-standstill, Iran is charging tolls, and the Islamabad talks on Saturday produced no formal agreement. The dominant themes for the week ahead are: (1) USD weakness — the stagflation premium is partially unwinding as oil retreats; (2) NZD outperformance — RBNZ hawkish surprise (discussed hiking at April AND May meetings) is the cleanest fundamental story in G10; (3) AUD recovery — high-beta risk-on beneficiary, copper at 3-week high, China PPI positive for first time in 3 years; (4) JPY vulnerability — energy importer, BOJ still accommodative, USD/JPY near 159; (5) USD PPI on Tuesday and GBP GDP on Thursday are the key data events.

currencies.name: US Dollar
currencies.rationale: USD lost its dominant stagflation premium as oil collapsed on the ceasefire announcement. DXY fell over 1.5% on the week, with the bulk of the move coming in a single session. The FOMC Minutes confirmed a hawkish hold — 'many said persistently higher oil prices could keep inflation elevated long enough to justify rate rises' — but this is now a conditional statement as oil retreats. Core PCE printed 0.4% MoM (hot), and CPI came in at 0.2% MoM (softer than feared). The net result is a USD that has lost its energy-driven haven premium but retains its rates support. Near-term weak bearish: the ceasefire removes the most acute USD tailwind, but the underlying stagflation story is not dead — any ceasefire breakdown would rapidly reverse the move. USD PPI on Tuesday is the key event.
drivers: Ceasefire removes oil-driven stagflation premium — DXY fell 1.5%+ on the week
FOMC Minutes hawkish hold: 'higher oil could justify rate rises' — conditional on oil staying elevated
Core PCE 0.4% MoM (hot beat) — stagflation framework intact but oil retreat dilutes impact
CPI 0.2% MoM (softer than feared) — reduces near-term urgency for Fed hike
USD PPI m/m + Core PPI m/m (Tue 14:30 FFT) — key event; hot print could reverse bearish bias

events.event: Core PPI m/m + PPI m/m (14:30 FFT)

currencies.name: Euro
currencies.rationale: EUR benefited from the oil collapse — as a net energy importer, lower Brent is a direct terms-of-trade improvement. EUR/USD recovered from sub-1.15 to above 1.17 on the ceasefire week. ECB hawkish repricing remains intact (two 25bp hikes priced by year-end), and Lagarde's speeches this week will be closely watched for any softening of tone given the oil retreat. German Industrial Production missed badly (-0.3% MoM vs +0.9% exp) and German Factory Orders disappointed, but these are backward-looking. Near-term weak bullish: the energy tailwind and ECB rates story provide support, but the structural fragility of the Eurozone (BTP-Bund spread, fiscal constraints) caps the upside. Lagarde speaks Tuesday and Wednesday.
drivers: Net energy importer — oil collapse from $116 to below $90/bbl is a direct EUR positive
ECB hawkish repricing intact: two 25bp hikes priced by year-end
EUR/USD recovered from sub-1.15 to above 1.17 on ceasefire week
German Industrial Production -0.3% MoM (miss) — backward-looking, not a fresh negative
Lagarde speaks Tue + Wed — watch for any softening given oil retreat

events.event: ECB Monetary Policy Meeting Accounts (13:30 FFT)

currencies.name: British Pound
currencies.rationale: Sterling was one of the week's outperformers — Cable broke 1.34 for the first time since the conflict began, driven by the ceasefire risk-on wave and the ongoing BOE hawkish repricing (~55bps of tightening priced, BofA forecasting two hikes in June and July). UK Final GDP Q4 was unrevised at 0.1% QoQ, and the Current Account beat expectations (-18.4B vs -23.3B exp). Near-term weak bullish: the BOE rates story and risk-on character both support GBP, and the GDP print on Thursday (February monthly GDP) is the key domestic catalyst. BOE Governor Bailey speaks Tuesday. GBP is the cleanest risk-on expression among the major European currencies.
drivers: Cable broke 1.34 — ceasefire risk-on wave + BOE hawkish repricing
BOE repricing: ~55bps of tightening priced; BofA forecasts two hikes (June + July)
UK Current Account beat (-18.4B vs -23.3B exp) — external balance improving
Risk-on character — GBP benefits from ceasefire and equity market recovery
GDP m/m (Thu 08:00 FFT) and BOE Bailey speaks (Tue) are the key events

currencies.name: Japanese Yen
currencies.rationale: JPY was the worst G10 performer on the ceasefire week — the currency that benefits most from safe-haven demand and energy shock fears was punished hardest when both reversed simultaneously. USD/JPY is near 159, well above the 155-157 range where verbal intervention was most active. Japan's energy import vulnerability means the JPY actually loses its haven appeal in a risk-on environment where oil is falling but equities are surging. BOJ remains accommodative relative to other G10 central banks, and the wide US-Japan yield differential persists. Near-term bearish: the ceasefire removes the key JPY support pillar (haven demand), and the risk-on environment favors high-beta currencies over defensive ones. No major JPY events this week.
drivers: Worst G10 performer on ceasefire week — lost both haven premium and energy shock support simultaneously
USD/JPY near 159 — above verbal intervention range (155-157); BOJ still accommodative
Energy import vulnerability means JPY loses appeal when oil falls + equities surge
Wide US-Japan yield differential persists — structural constraint on JPY strength
No major JPY events this week — pure sentiment and yield differential play

currencies.name: Australian Dollar
currencies.rationale: AUD staged a sharp recovery on the ceasefire week — as a high-beta, risk-sensitive currency it was one of the primary beneficiaries of the global risk-on wave. Copper hit a 3-week high, China PPI turned positive for the first time in over three years (0.1% YoY), and the RBA is still repriced toward tightening (~62bps by year-end). AUD/USD recovered from 0.62 handle to above 0.64. Near-term weak bullish: the risk-on recovery is real but the ceasefire is fragile — any breakdown would rapidly reverse AUD gains. Australian Employment Change and Unemployment Rate on Thursday are the key domestic catalysts.
drivers: High-beta risk-on recovery — primary beneficiary of ceasefire wave alongside NZD
Copper at 3-week high — commodity terms of trade improving
China PPI +0.1% YoY (first positive in 3+ years) — AUD/NZD positive via China channel
RBA repriced to ~62bps of tightening by year-end — rates support intact
Employment Change + Unemployment Rate (Thu 03:30 FFT) — key domestic catalyst

currencies.name: Canadian Dollar
currencies.rationale: CAD was the biggest loser of the ceasefire week among commodity currencies — oil collapsed 16% (WTI from $105 to below $88/bbl), directly undermining the petro-proxy support that had been CAD's primary tailwind. Canada's Employment Change missed expectations (actual -7.4K vs +15K exp), and the Services PMI remained in contraction at 47.2. The BoC has been repriced to ~44bps of tightening but this is driven by imported inflation, not domestic growth momentum. Near-term weak bearish: the oil collapse removes the key CAD support pillar, and the soft jobs data adds further pressure. No major CAD events this week.
drivers: Oil collapsed 16% on ceasefire — WTI from $105 to below $88/bbl; petro-proxy support removed
Employment Change -7.4K (miss vs +15K exp) — soft jobs data adds pressure
Services PMI 47.2 — still in contraction; domestic demand weak
BoC repriced to ~44bps of tightening — imported inflation, not domestic strength
No major CAD events this week — pure oil and risk sentiment proxy

currencies.name: Swiss Franc
currencies.rationale: CHF had a mixed but ultimately stable week — it benefited late in the week as ceasefire uncertainty crept back in, but lagged badly during the peak risk-on phase when funds rotated aggressively into high-beta alternatives. The Franc's asymmetric haven behavior was fully exposed: it tends to be left behind during strong risk-on moves but recovers when uncertainty returns. SNB intervention cap remains intact, and the market still prefers USD as the cleaner defensive currency given its yield advantage. Near-term neutral: neither a strong positive nor negative driver dominates. SNB Chairman Schlegel speaks Wednesday, and Swiss PPI on Thursday are the key events.
drivers: Asymmetric haven — lagged badly in risk-on but recovered as ceasefire uncertainty returned
SNB intervention cap intact — limits excessive CHF strength
USD preferred as haven over CHF — yield advantage and intervention risk
Swiss domestic calendar virtually empty — pure external sentiment and USD dynamics
SNB Schlegel speaks Wed + Swiss PPI Thu are the key events

currencies.name: New Zealand Dollar
currencies.rationale: NZD was the G10 outperformer for two consecutive sessions — the RBNZ hawkish surprise at the April meeting was the catalyst. Governor Breman confirmed the Board discussed hiking at the April AND May meetings, and markets now price 75bps of tightening by year-end (up 15bps since last week). NZD/USD surged to the 0.58 handle. The combination of a hawkish central bank surprise and the broader risk-on ceasefire wave created a powerful double tailwind. Near-term bullish: the RBNZ repricing story is fresh and not fully priced, and NZD benefits from both the risk-on environment and the domestic rates story. Governor Breman speaks Wednesday — any further hawkish signals would extend the rally.
drivers: RBNZ hawkish surprise — discussed hiking at April AND May meetings; 75bps priced by year-end
G10 outperformer for two consecutive sessions — ceasefire risk-on + RBNZ double tailwind
NZD/USD surged to 0.58 handle — fresh multi-week highs
Risk-on character — benefits from ceasefire and equity market recovery
RBNZ Governor Breman speaks Wed — key catalyst for further repricing
